title: Crustless Mushroom and Cheese Quiche Bake

subs.name: full-fat curd (hung briefly to remove whey) — gives a slightly tangier custard
subs.notes: use the same quantity; the texture will be a little denser

subs.name: crumbled paneer (75 g) — does not melt the same way but gives a pleasant texture and protein
subs.notes: mix half into the custard and crumble rest on top

subs.name: broccoli florets (blanched), peas, or diced capsicum — use any combination of vegetables
subs.notes: make sure to cook out excess moisture before adding

ingredients.name: fresh spinach, roughly chopped
visual_checks: about 80 g; adds colour and nutrition
ingredients.notes: about 80 g; adds colour and nutrition

subs.name: fenugreek leaves (methi) — add a wonderful earthy bitterness; wilt briefly first
subs.notes: use about 50 g fresh methi leaves

steps.text: Preheat your oven to 180°C. Grease a 22 cm ovenproof skillet or shallow baking dish generously with a little butter and set aside.

Melt the butter in a frying pan over medium heat. Sauté the onion for 3 minutes until soft, then add the garlic and cook 1 minute more. Add the mushrooms and cook for 5–6 minutes, stirring occasionally, until they lose their moisture and turn golden. Add the spinach and stir until wilted, about 2 minutes. Season with salt, pepper and strip in the thyme leaves if using. Spread evenly in the prepared baking dish.

In a large bowl, whisk together the eggs, cream, milk, nutmeg, salt and pepper until fully combined and slightly frothy. Stir in half the grated cheese. Pour the egg mixture over the vegetables in the baking dish.

Scatter the remaining cheese evenly over the top. Place the dish in the preheated oven and bake for 30–35 minutes, until the top is golden and the custard is just set — a gentle wobble in the very centre is fine, as residual heat will finish it.
Remove from the oven and rest for 10 minutes before cutting. The quiche will settle as it cools and slices more cleanly. Scatter parsley over the top and serve warm or at room temperature.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: cream → full-fat curd (hung briefly to remove whey) — gives a slightly tangier custard (use the same quantity; the texture will be a little denser); mushroom → broccoli florets (blanched), peas, or diced capsicum — use any combination of vegetables (make sure to cook out excess moisture before adding); cheese → crumbled paneer (75 g) — does not melt the same way but gives a pleasant texture and protein (mix half into the custard and crumble rest on top); spinach → fenugreek leaves (methi) — add a wonderful earthy bitterness; wilt briefly first (use about 50 g fresh methi leaves)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: If you have an oven, use it at 180°C exactly as written. Without an oven, use a pressure cooker as a steam oven: grease the inner vessel, pour in the egg mixture with the vegetables, cover with a plate (not the weight), and cook on low flame for 20–25 minutes until the custard is set — check at 20 minutes. The result is slightly denser but delicious. Amul cheddar block (grated) works perfectly as the cheese.
